summary: Do not ship: R-43 is incomplete. The frozen RED/GREEN lock is mechanically intact but permits reconciliation to silently trust contradictory ref OIDs.

findings.title: Blocker: reconciliation drops the ref OID at its observation seam
findings.body: `enumerateNsRefs` and `enumerateSiblingRefs` retain the OID observed by `ls-remote`, but `observeBranchPr(head)` accepts only the branch name and checks only whether GraphQL reports the ref as present. If `ls-remote` observes OID A while a stale GraphQL view reports present OID B with an empty PR connection, this returns `pr:null`; reconciliation exits successfully although an armed PR on A may have been hidden. The trusted-PR path similarly compares OIDs only for create/adopt, so it can retain or add auto-merge using ownership proof for a stale head. This is new evidence beyond R-43's handled `ref:null` case. The committed fixture is blind to it: sibling GraphQL responses default to OID `111…`, while sibling refs normally use `333…`, yet those reconciliation cases expect success.
findings.recommendation: Pass the enumerated OID into the shared observation seam and require GraphQL ref OID, `ls-remote` OID, and any trusted PR `headRefOid` to agree before retaining or granting authorization. Treat mismatches as `revocationBlind`, reject OID changes across pagination pages, and make `RefObs` a true discriminated union. Update the sibling stub to derive its normal OID from `STUB_SIBLINGS`, add an explicit reconciliation OID-mismatch witness, then rebuild the fixed-test RED/GREEN records.

next_steps: Complete the OID consistency invariant across create/adopt, trusted-PR, sibling, and reconciliation paths.
Strengthen the frozen tests and regenerate verification records without weakening existing assertions.
